top_categories.workout_context: Race-pace efforts that mimic Granfondo Via del Sale's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before March.
At mile 60 of 97, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
75ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
7,260ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Granfondo Via del Sale. Train it until it feels boring.
Granfondo Via del Sale's paved Italian provincial roads from the Adriatic forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 97 miles.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 7,260ft of climbing on paved Italian provincial roads from the Adriatic does to your power file on race day.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 97 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
At Granfondo Via del Sale, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 97 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Tempo is Granfondo Via del Sale's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 97 miles on paved Italian provincial roads from the Adriatic. Train it until it's automatic.
The aerobic base that lets you ride paved Italian provincial roads from the Adriatic at tempo for 97 miles without cracking. No shortcuts.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Granfondo Via del Sale’s 97 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Race Simulation, Durability, and TT Threshold to prepare you for 97 miles of paved italian provincial roads from the adriatic coast through romagna hills in Cervia, Emilia-Romagna, Italy.
